insight: Shadowfang's click ability should not be used until you are completely done building Shadowfangs, since using it prematurely (e.g., against a first Wincer shot) wastes the production window for making more Shadowfangs.
author: xyotsuba
context: Discussion of red unit strategy and how Mobile Animus interacts with various red units including Shadowfang.

insight: Tia Threnody suffers from a drawback in high-inflation states where Tarsiers are weaker, but pairing it with a non-committed (mobile) Animus source can mitigate this by avoiding full Animus investment.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of Mobile Animus synergies, specifically whether it enables Tia Threnody lines that would otherwise be awkward.

insight: Animus is the least-purchased tech building overall; any unit that increases the frequency of viable red strategies would therefore have an outsized positive impact on set diversity.
author: extratricky
context: Community discussion about whether a mobile/flexible Animus unit would make red more interesting to play.

insight: Shiver Yeti at 5R is very mediocre in most sets but is a must-buy in sets with Plexo Cell, Centurion, or other units that make Chill 2 particularly valuable; its niche role means it likely does not need a buff.
author: consensus
context: Extended expert discussion among mtanzer, extratricky, masn6811, and xyotsuba about Shiver Yeti's power level and potential cost changes.

insight: Shiver Yeti is also used as a late-game Rhino substitute to make gambits harder in almost any set, not only in sets where Chill is actively exploited.
author: extratricky
context: Discussion of how broadly Shiver Yeti sees play even in sets where it is not a primary strategy.

insight: A defender facing multiple Shiver Yetis can intentionally keep fewer 2-HP defenders than the opponent's Yeti count, ensuring the freeze is wasted on an Engineer rather than a valuable blocker.
author: .bky_1556
context: Expert discussion on how defenders can arrange their board to minimise the value of Shiver Yeti's Chill.

insight: Cryo Ray is often better than 1 attack because it can exploit the opponent when not breaching: the freeze can be converted into effective threat on the next turn, similar to how Shiver Yeti's Chill can be used offensively.
author: extratricky
context: Comparison of Chill-based units and their offensive utility during the Shiver Yeti balance discussion.

insight: Corpus provides 2 soak for 4R plus a Husk and a click ability; a hypothetical 4R unit with 2 soak and no other text would be comparable to Corpus minus those extras and would be solid red soak, well above the typical red efficiency of 2.5 gold per soak.
author: consensus
context: Discussion comparing red soak efficiency using Corpus as a benchmark during Shiver Yeti cost analysis.

insight: In Doomed Drone sets, P2 opening D1E (Drone turn 1, Engineer turn 2) is a strong general opening used when the set is not high-econ enough for DD/DDEE and the player does not want a turn-2 Conduit or Animus; Doomed Drones bought before they generate defensive value are still nearly as good as regular Drones.
author: redrame
context: Players discussing why D1E is commonly seen as P2 in Doomed Drone sets.

insight: In Prismata, early-game investment is disproportionately valuable due to economic inflation: a tech building bought late can cost up to 8 gold of lost value, and even a single floated gold or an inefficient Conduit timing can be punished decisively at high levels of play.
author: steel0229e
context: Expert discussion between steel0229e and jeacaveo about the relative importance of opening turns versus late-game decisions.

insight: Recovering from a weak early turn requires approximately 3–5 perfectly played turns by the trailing player plus a mistake from the opponent; because everyone makes small inefficiencies, comebacks are possible but demand near-perfect execution.
author: steel0229e
context: Steel discussing the snowball nature of Prismata's economy and what it takes to come back from an early disadvantage.

insight: Creating deliberate complexity on the board in a position where one side has a clean forcing line is an important skill: sufficiently complicated board states shift the outcome toward tactical execution rather than opening theory, giving the stronger tactician a path to recover.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion of how to play from a disadvantaged position and when strategic complexity benefits the trailing player.

insight: Master Bot does not know how to take full advantage of extra gold in the early game; giving MB a 5-gold handicap is reportedly easier than giving the same handicap to Adept Bot, which still plays sensibly when you skip your first turn.
author: awaclus
context: Players comparing difficulty of handicap games against Master Bot versus Adept Bot.

insight: When casual bots (Rush Bot or Breachproof Bot) receive a favorable unit pool in min-unit-count games, they can be harder to beat than Master Bot; Master Bot is better at tactics than strategy, making it more dangerous in smaller sets where strategy matters less.
author: p0lari
context: Discussion of how to get more challenging bot games by adjusting the unit count slider in casual queue.

insight: Players prefer VS Computer mode over casual queue bot games specifically to ban units that cause Master Bot to make poor decisions, implying there are known unit types or sets where MB plays especially badly.
author: randomflyingtaco
context: Discussion about matchmaking options and why some players prefer the VS Computer lobby over casual queue when playing against bots.

insight: Common Prismata strategic archetypes include high-econ, breachproof (BP), and rush; these are not mutually exclusive (e.g., high-econ transitioning into a breachproof endgame). Unit-specific rushes are named after the target unit (e.g., 'Tatsu rush').
author: consensus
context: New player asking about named playstyle archetypes analogous to deck names in other card games.
